---
title: "Environmental and predator impacts on Antarctic Krill (*Euphausia superba*) population dynamics from an integrated length-to-age assessment model perspective"
subtitle: "Version 2 - Revised"
author:
  - Mauricio Mardones ^[Universidad de Magallanes, mamardon@umag.cl; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile; Centro IDEAL, Chile; Instituto Español de Oceanografía, Spain]
  - Erica Jarvis Mason^[Antarctic División, SouthWest NOAA, La Jolla, California, USA]
  - Andrea Pinones ^[Universidad Austral de Chile; Centro IDEAL, Chile; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile]
  - Lucas Krüger ^[Instituto Antártico Chileno, Instituto Milenio Base, Chile]
  - Francisco Santa Cruz ^[Instituto Antártico Chileno, Universidad de Magallanes, Chile; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile]
  - César Cárdenas^[Instituto Antártico Chileno; Instituto Milenio Base, Chile]
  - Richard Methot ^[Fishery Science División, NorthWeast NOAA, Seattle, USA]
output:
  bookdown::word_document2:
    reference_docx: MS2_Krill_template.docx
    number_sections: false
fig_caption: yes
bibliography: bib/SA_krill.bib
csl: bib/apa.csl
#csl: icesjournal.csl
link-citations: yes
toc: false
linkcolor: blue
linestretch: 1.5
header-includes:
- \fontsize{12}{16}
- \selectfont
- \usepackage{lscape}
---

\newpage
\tableofcontents
\newpage

```{r setup1, echo=FALSE}
rm(list = ls())
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval=FALSE,
                      echo = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      fig.show='hold',
                      dev = 'jpeg',
                      dpi = 300,
                      tidy.opts=list(width.cutoff=50),
                      tidy=TRUE)
```

```{r message=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
library(here)
library(kableExtra)
library(ggpubr)
library(tibble)
library(readxl)
library(openxlsx)
library(r4ss)
library(readr)
library(flextable)
library(officer)
library(r4ss)
```

\newpage

# Graphical abstract

```{r eval=TRUE, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Trade-off between parsimony and realism in krill stock assessment. Models that incorporate ecosystem drivers (environmental variability and predator effects) into the population dynamics are more ecologically realistic but may have poorer statistical performance than models that ignore these drivers."}
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("Figs", "graphical_abstract_krill_v2.png"))
```


# Abstract

Understanding the population dynamics of Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*) is fundamental for ecosystem-based fisheries management in the Western Antarctic Peninsula, where environmental variability and predator–prey interactions are known to strongly influence population productivity. However, most stock assessment frameworks applied to krill and other exploited marine resources rely on model-based approaches in which ecosystem drivers are not explicitly embedded within the core population dynamics. These drivers are typically explored through external sensitivity analyses or post-hoc diagnostics, thereby limiting their influence on estimated stock status and management advice. In this study, a spatially implicit integrated length-to-age assessment model is applied to Antarctic krill, allowing ecosystem drivers to be incorporated directly into the population dynamics. Within a Stock Synthesis–based framework, alternative model configurations are evaluated in which environmental covariates and predator indices are either explicitly linked to key state processes (recruitment and natural mortality) or excluded from the structural equations. This design allows us to assess their effects on recruitment, total biomass, spawning stock biomass, and fishing mortality. Systematic and coherent differences are observed among model configurations. When environmental variability and predator effects are embedded within the state equations, lower estimates of recruitment, total biomass, and spawning biomass are obtained, together with altered estimates of fishing mortality, relative to models in which ecosystem drivers are ignored. In addition, poorer statistical performance is generally observed for these ecosystem-informed models, as indicated by higher RMSE values, more pronounced retrospective patterns, and more structured residual patterns. This deterioration in fit is interpreted as the result of the increased informational and structural cost incurred by the model when additional components are imposed, rather than as evidence against the relevance of the ecological mechanisms themselves. Overall, these results indicate that stock assessments excluding ecosystem processes may overestimate population productivity and stock status by prioritizing statistical fit over ecosystem realism. Despite the challenges associated with integrating ecosystem information into fully integrated assessment models, it is demonstrated that environmental and predator effects can be consistently incorporated within the core population dynamics of krill assessments. The direct embedding of these drivers in the state equations provides a more precautionary and ecologically realistic representation of krill population dynamics, and strengthens the scientific basis for management advice consistent with CCAMLR's objectives for conservation and sustainable use in the Western Antarctic Peninsula.

*Keywords: Antarctic krill, population dynamics, length-to-age model, stock-recruitment, ecosystem variables, model performance, fisheries management, CCAMLR*

\newpage

# Introduction

Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*, hereafter krill) constitutes one of the largest populations on the planet in terms of biomass and serves as a keystone species in the Antarctic ecosystem [@Atkinson2019a; @McBride2021; @Kawaguchi2024]. The population is predominantly concentrated in the Western Antarctic Peninsula (WAP), being the primary area for krill harvesting over the past four decades and also an area that is a critical feeding ground for predators such as penguins, seals, and whales [@Watters2013; @Hill2016; @Gallagher2023a]. At the same time, the WAP is one of the regions most severely affected by climate change, experiencing rapid increases in ocean temperature, sea ice loss, and shifts in primary productivity [@Turner2005; @Lima2013; @Atkinson2019a; @Morley2020; @Carrasco2021; @McBride2021]. These overlapping pressures have intensified scientific interest in understanding krill population dynamics from both an ecological and a fisheries perspective in such a complex and rapidly changing environment [@Warwick-Evans2022; @Kawaguchi2024].

Given the importance of krill to the ecosystem and its commercial value, effective management of krill stocks is essential for maintaining the balance of the Antarctic ecosystem while supporting sustainable fisheries. Recognizing this, the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR) has been developing a revised ecosystem-based approach [@Constable2011; @Watters2013; @Cavanagh2016; @Constable2023; @Zhao2023; @Kawaguchi2024]. This strategy seeks to balance commercial harvesting with a precautionary framework that safeguards krill-dependent species across spatial and temporal scales. It comprises three key components: (i) stock assessments to establish precautionary harvest rates, (ii) periodic updates of krill biomass estimates at multiple spatial scales, and (iii) spatial overlap analysis to guide the allocation of catches and mitigate ecosystem-wide risks [@Warwick-Evans2022; @Kawaguchi2024].

To provide scientific advice on the status and abundance of krill, CCAMLR has historically relied on dedicated scientific surveys [@Watters2013; @Hill2019; @Dornam2021; @Green2023]. However, changes in survey design-both methodological and spatial-have raised concerns about the reliability of these estimates, while the high costs associated with such surveys have further motivated the search for alternative stock estimation methods. More recently, and as part of its new strategy, CCAMLR has explored a variety of model-based (indirect) approaches to assess krill stock status and support fisheries management decisions [@Hill2019]. Some of these stock assessment models have used a single source of data-either fishery-dependent catches [@Wang2021] or survey-based monitoring programs [@Kinzey2011; @Kinzey2019; @Green2023]. At present, the Generalized Yield Model (GRYM) [@Maschette2020] is a model-based approach used by CCAMLR to generate management recommendations, allowing adjustments to the decision rules to predefined catch limits set by CCAMLR [@Constable2000; @CCAMLR2010; @CCAMLR2021a]. However, model-based approaches exhibit important limitations, including the effective integration of multiple data sources, the incorporation of ecosystem-level variables into population dynamics, and the capacity to account for the high spatial and temporal variability intrinsic to krill productivity [@Meyer2020; @Trathan2021].

Increasing evidence demonstrates that environmental conditions, such as sea surface temperature, sea ice extent, and phytoplankton availability, strongly influence krill recruitment, survival, and spatial distribution [@Murphy2007; @Flores2012; @Saba2014; @Morley2020; @Perry2020; @Walsh2020; @Vorrath2020; @Veytia2021]. Similarly, fluctuations in predator abundance and distribution can exert significant top-down control on krill populations [@Moosa2017; @Reisinger2022; @Baines2022; @Riaz2023; @Biuw2024]. There is now broad scientific consensus on the need for stock assessment models that incorporate multiple data sources, capture temporal and spatial variability, and explicitly account for ecosystem interactions [@Marshall2019]. Neglecting these ecosystem drivers can lead to biased estimates of stock status and suboptimal management advice [@Haltuch2009; @Richards2016; @Marshall2019; @Crone2019; @Robertson2024]. Stock assessment frameworks aligned with CCAMLR's new strategy should therefore explicitly incorporate these ecosystem components to more accurately represent krill population processes.

Integrating ecological complexity into krill stock assessments presents multiple challenges. Long-term data collection is frequently affected by changes in monitoring programs and sampling methodologies [@Hill2019], and some key regions of the WAP lack sufficient temporal coverage to construct reliable population indices. Indirect ecosystem drivers, such as environmental variability and predator–prey interactions, are particularly difficult to quantify, given the spatial and temporal heterogeneity of krill dynamics, their non-linear responses to environmental forcing, and the simultaneous influence of multiple stressors [@Constable2023]. These constraints have historically limited the structural complexity of krill assessments and the degree to which ecosystem processes are embedded within their core population dynamics. Despite these challenges, a substantial and diverse body of data on krill and their associated environment has accumulated over several decades. Fishery monitoring programs provide detailed biological data from catches through onboard scientific observers, notably through CCAMLR's Scheme of International Scientific Observation (SISO). Hydroacoustic biomass surveys, primarily led by the United States and China, represent the longest-running and spatially broadest independent assessment efforts in the Southern Ocean [@Hill2019; @Dornam2021; @Green2023]. Ecosystem monitoring programs such as the CCAMLR Ecosystem Monitoring Program (CEMP) systematically track predator populations and environmental variability at key sites across the WAP. Together, these complementary data streams provide the informational foundation needed to move beyond data-limited, single-source assessments toward integrated frameworks that explicitly account for the coupled biological, ecological, and fishery processes shaping krill populations.

CCAMLR has traditionally relied on assessments conducted within a data-limited context using relatively simple model-based approaches, such as GRYM models, which do not explicitly incorporate environmental or ecological variables in the representation of population dynamics. In contrast, a wide range of integrated assessment frameworks now allow the incorporation of diverse data sources and increasing levels of structural complexity. These include spatially explicit approaches such as the State-Space Assessment Model (SAM) [@Nielsen2021] and the Globally Applicable Area-Disaggregated General Ecosystem Toolbox (Gadget) [@Bartolino2011]. For statistical catch-at-age assessments, widely used frameworks include the C++ Algorithmic Stock Assessment Laboratory (CASAL) [@Bull2004] and Stock Synthesis (SS3) [@Methot2013], among others. Notably, Stock Synthesis can also be applied to species that are difficult to age, such as Antarctic krill, through integrated length-to-age transformation models that explicitly link observed length compositions to underlying age-structured population dynamics. These advanced tools provide robust and flexible methodologies to address data limitations while accommodating complex biological processes and ecosystem interactions, thereby enabling a more realistic representation of population dynamics and improved scientific support for ecosystem-based fisheries management [@Hoyle2020].

This study pursues two main objectives. The first is to integrate multiple data sources on Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*), including fishery operations (e.g., catches and effort distribution), biological characteristics (e.g., growth rates and reproductive parameters), spatiotemporal population dynamics, and key ecosystem drivers (e.g., environmental forcing and predator–prey interactions), into a unified stock assessment framework. Specifically, we apply an integrated length-to-age model that enables the explicit inclusion of environmental covariates affecting biomass, recruitment patterns, and overall productivity, as well as the potential influence of predator populations through the configuration of natural mortality. The second objective is to evaluate the performance of alternative model configurations representing different hypotheses regarding ecosystem influences, using diagnostic tools and performance metrics commonly applied in contemporary stock assessments. Consistent with CCAMLR's ecosystem-based management framework [@Constable2011; @Cavanagh2016; @Hill2019; @Constable2023; @Zhao2023; @CCAMLRWGSAM2024; @WGEMM2024], this approach provides empirical evidence of the value of ecosystem-informed assessment models, demonstrating that the incorporation of environmental and predator covariates influences population variables, model fit, predictive skill, and structural uncertainty, with implications for precautionary and sustainable management advice for the Antarctic krill fishery.

\newpage

# Methodology

## Study Area

The study area covers the WAP, corresponding to FAO Statistical Subarea 48.1, one of the main regions for krill fishing activity and where CCAMLR is actively developing a revised management approach at a finer spatial scale. To enhance the spatial resolution of our analysis of krill population dynamics, five Management Units (MUs) proposed by CCAMLR were used: Bransfield Strait, Elephant Island, Gerlache Strait, Joinville Island, and the Southwest sector [@Dornam2021] (S1 Fig. 1). Although these management units were not originally designed to incorporate krill biological or population-specific characteristics [@Watters2013; @Constable2023; @Zaldua2024a], their subdivision allows for finer-scale examination of krill data, enhancing spatial resolution and facilitating the identification of heterogeneity among different data sources, including fishery-dependent and environmental information, thereby supporting a more detailed characterization of krill population dynamics. To represent spatial heterogeneity in krill dynamics, we implemented a spatially implicit modeling approach based on the "areas-as-fleets" framework [@Waterhouse2014; @Punt2019; @Nielsen2021], in which each MU of the WAP is treated as a distinct fleet with independently estimated selectivity, catchability (*q*), and abundance indices. This structure is particularly appropriate for krill, given the well-documented spatial variability in population processes and fishing effort across the WAP [@Perry2019; @Perry2020; @Reiss2020; @Veytia2021; @SantaCruz2022; @Mardones2026], including known differences in size composition among MUs [@Kinzey2011; @Reiss2020; @Cutter2022]. Survey indices and biological compositions (e.g., length-frequency data) are assigned to their corresponding MU based on sampling location [@Dornam2021]; fishery-dependent data are stratified by MU according to reported fishing effort. This disaggregated observation structure captures regional variability without requiring explicit movement modeling [@Punt2019]. Although MUs are treated as separate fleets for observational purposes, core population processes (recruitment, growth, and a single annual spawning pulse between January and February [@Perry2019; @McBride2021]) are modeled collectively, representing the krill stock as a single interconnected population within Subarea 48.1.


## Length-to-Age Model Description

Like many invertebrates, krill present challenges for age determination, which complicates modeling population dynamics. Most modern stock assessments rely on age-structured modeling approaches to estimate marine population parameters [@Punt2013]. Although recent advances have improved krill aging techniques [@Kilada2017], such methods have historically been unavailable for krill. Consequently, stock assessment for krill typically uses length data to approximate age structure through transition matrices. These matrices estimate the probability that individuals of a given length belong to specific age classes [@Laslett2004; @Lee2019; @Rudd2021; @Zhang2022; @Lee2024]. Given the scarcity or absence of direct age data in krill monitoring programs, length-based models offer a practical alternative to age-structured approaches. To date, all model-based krill assessments have used length-to-age frameworks [@Kinzey2015; @Kinzey2019; @Wang2021; @Green2023; @Zhang2022]. Integrated models can effectively capture age structure by transforming length observations into population-level dynamics [@Punt2013; @Lee2024]. Length data, which are cost-effective and readily available [@Chong2019; @Canales2021], also provide valuable insights into krill population structure due to their correlation with age [@Thanassekos2014].

To perform this transformation, models typically use Age-Length Keys (ALKs). An ALK is a probabilistic matrix that estimates the likelihood of individuals of a given length belonging to specific age classes. This method assumes that both aged and measured individuals are random samples from the same population and should be applied within the same time period to avoid biases [@ICCA2003; @Punt2003; @Punt2013; @Lee2024]. Using this statistical framework, we constructed the ALK for Antarctic krill, which was subsequently applied within the assessment model (S1 Fig. 3). In the catch-at-length stock assessment model, growth was parameterized using a von Bertalanffy growth function (VBGF) for length-at-age. This formulation was integrated with the weight-at-length relationship, which was assumed known and derived from the parameters reported by @Maschette2020 for Antarctic krill. To account for early ontogeny, linear growth was assumed below a predefined settlement age of 1.5 years. Maturity was modeled as an age-based logistic function, with the onset of maturity occurring between 2 and 3 years [@Thanassekos2014]. Fishery selectivity was represented by a logistic function parameterized by the length at 50% selectivity ($L_{50}$) and the selection range ($L_{95}$), estimated independently for each MU to account for spatial differences in size-dependent catchability. Survey selectivity was modeled using the same logistic formulation, likewise estimated separately per MU. Allowing fleet, and MU-specific selectivity curves is particularly important for krill, where spatial variation in size composition, driven by environmental gradients and population structure, can produce systematic differences in the size of individuals encountered by different gears and in different regions (S1 Fig. 5). Initial parameter values and bounds used for estimation are provided in S2, Table 1.

The stock-recruitment (SR) relationship was modeled using a Beverton–Holt function (S2, Eq. 7), following @Kinzey2019. The steepness parameter was fixed at 0.85 to maintain consistency with previous studies, reflecting the weak relationship between spawning biomass and recruitment in krill populations, where recruitment is largely driven by environmental variability [@Dong2025; @Kinzey2019; @Perry2020]. Unfished recruitment ($R_0$) was estimated assuming a mean recruitment of 23 mm (log-scale), with a prior of 10. Recruitment deviations were included to capture interannual variability using the deviation vector approach (*recdev = 1*), whereby recruitment is modeled as a function of spawning stock biomass plus a deviation term. Cyclic recruitment patterns were also incorporated, assuming periodicity at three- and five-year intervals [@Perry2019; @McBride2021; @Kinzey2019; @Thanassekos2014]. All life-history parameters and assumptions were specified as priors to define the initial population dynamics, based on data from @Smith2023a, @Maschette2020, and @Kinzey2011. These biological parameters, together with the initial selectivity assumptions, are summarized in Table 1 S1.

The statistical model for krill was configured using Stock Synthesis v3.30.21 (SS3) [@Methot2013]. SS3 is a structured stock assessment model belonging to the class of integrated stock assessment models, with a set of sub-models that simulate growth, maturity, fecundity, recruitment, and mortality processes, along with observation sub-models that generate expected values for different data types. The model is coded in Automatic Differentiation Model Builder (ADMB) [@Fournier2012; @Methot2013; @methot2020stock] with estimated parameters. SS3 model configuration files are available in the code repository (Supporting Information 1). All modeling analyses were conducted in R version 4.4.2 [@R-base], with visualization and diagnostics performed was made through *r4ss* [@Taylor2019] and *ss3diags* [@Winker2023] packages. The complete set of equations governing population dynamics, selectivity, stock-recruitment, mortality, and parameter estimation within the SS3 framework are detailed in Supplementary Material 2.

## Environmental Link Formulation

Within the Stock Synthesis (SS3) framework, environmental covariates influence population dynamics through link functions applied to underlying model parameters (e.g., recruitment, natural mortality, or growth), rather than acting directly on observed quantities [@methot2020stock; @Caserta2025]. These parameter-level effects propagate to derived quantities such as annual recruitment. Let $P_t$ denote a generic model parameter influenced by the environment, $P_{base}$ its baseline value under average environmental conditions, $E_t$ the environmental covariate, and $\beta_E$ the estimable coefficient describing environmental sensitivity. Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) was selected as the environmental covariate based on its well-documented influence on krill population dynamics in the Western Antarctic Peninsula [@Flores2012; @Morley2020; @Veytia2021; @Walsh2020], and its demonstrated effect on krill recruitment productivity specifically confirmed by @Saba2014 and @Mardones2025. Chl-a was obtained from a combination of satellite products including *Nimbus-7* and the *EU Copernicus Marine Service*. Prior to model fitting, the Chl-a time series was standardised to zero mean and unit variance:

$$E_t = \frac{C_t - \bar{C}}{\sigma_C}$$

where $C_t$ is the raw annual Chl-a concentration (mg m$^{-3}$), and $\bar{C}$ and $\sigma_C$ are the historical mean and standard deviation. This standardisation improves numerical stability, renders $\beta_E$ interpretable as the change in the target parameter per one standard deviation anomaly in Chl-a, and is consistent with recommended practice in SS3 [@methot2020stock]. Under this formulation, the environmental link captures interannual variability in recruitment relative to the long-term baseline $\ln(R_0)_{base}$. The environmental effect was implemented via an additive link (link type 2 in SS3) [@Caserta2025]:

$$P_t = P_{\text{base}} + \beta_E \cdot E_t$$

where $\beta_E$ is estimated freely within the model. For years with missing Chl-a observations, SS3 assigns $E_t = 0$, effectively reverting the parameter to its baseline value [@methot2020stock].

## Predator Modelling

Predation is incorporated in SS3 through an additional mortality component ($M_2$), augmenting background natural mortality ($M_1$) [@methot2020stock]. This approach enables partial representation of ecosystem processes within a single-species framework, in contrast to fully dynamic ecosystem models such as ATLANTIS or Ecopath with Ecosim [@fulton2003; @fulton2011; @christensen2004]. Total instantaneous mortality is defined as:

$$Z_{a,t} = M1_{a,t} + \sum_i M2_{a,t,i} + F_{a,t}$$

where $M2_{a,t,i}$ represents predation mortality from predator $i$. In our krill assessment, predation pressure was represented using the three main penguin species in the Antarctic Peninsula: Adélie (*Pygoscelis adeliae*), Chinstrap (*Pygoscelis antarcticus*), and Gentoo (*Pygoscelis papua*), whose annual abundance was obtained from 399 colonies monitored over the period 2000–2020 through the Mapping Application for Penguin Populations and Projected Dynamics ([MAPPPD](https://www.penguinmap.com/mapppd/sources/)) [@Oceanites2021]. Penguin abundance was aggregated across species and used as a combined predator index. Length-frequency distributions of krill from penguin stomach contents, used to characterise predator size selectivity, were obtained from the U.S.AMLR Scientific Research Program (AMLR), with data available from 1992 onwards. The predator component was implemented as a dedicated fleet within SS3, combining two observational data streams: (i) the aggregated penguin abundance index, used to inform time-varying predation mortality, and (ii) the annual krill length-frequency distributions from stomach contents, used to estimate predator selectivity. Predation mortality was parameterized as:

$$M2_{a,t} = Q \times I_{pred,t}$$

where $I_{pred,t}$ is the standardized predator index and $Q$ is an estimable scaling parameter, analogous to catchability in fishing mortality [@methot2020stock]. The term $M2_{a,t}$ was further parameterised as a time-varying quantity via annual log-scale deviations with a zero-reverting logit-link random walk (dev\_link = 5 in SS3), which constrains $M2_{a,t}$ within biologically plausible bounds across the time series. Diet composition data, in the form of annual length-frequency distributions of krill from penguin stomach contents, informed the size-selectivity function of predation. A two-parameter logistic selectivity was used:

$$S(l) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\ln(19) \cdot (l - \mu) / \delta}}$$

where $\mu$ is the length at 50% selectivity and $\delta$ is the 95% width of the ascending limb. Parameters were estimated within SS3 using the stomach content length data as the observational likelihood component for the predator fleet, allowing the model to reconcile abundance and diet data streams during estimation [@methot2020stock]. Model diagnostics for predation focus on the relative contribution of predation to total mortality, summarized by the ratio $M2/Z$, as well as the shape of the estimated selectivity function, which reveals size-dependent predation patterns. The M2 framework relies on simplifying assumptions, including the absence of predator–prey feedbacks, a linear functional response between predator abundance and predation mortality, and the use of population averaged selectivity.

## Data Sources

Along with data on predators and environmental conditions described previously, a primary input to the model was derived from fishery-dependent data and scientific monitoring programs. Fishery-dependent data were obtained from the CCAMLR Krill Fishery Monitoring Program and the SISO Scheme, including total catches in weight, effort (grouped by vessel, area, and depth), and krill length-frequency distributions. Length data were standardized using a fixed bin structure of 28 length intervals, each representing the lower edge of 0.2 cm width bins from 1.2 to 6.6 cm. This ensured consistency across years, MUs, and fleets (fishery, survey, and predator), facilitating reliable temporal and spatial comparisons of size structure, covering the period from 1990 to 2020. Fishery-independent data originated from U.S. AMLR Program acoustic surveys and included standardized biomass indices conducted between 1997 and 2011 by MU [@Dornam2021]. The discontinuation of biological sampling after 2011 reflects the transition of the AMLR monitoring programme towards autonomous underwater vehicle glider-based acoustic surveys [Reiss2021]. Consequently, length-frequency data from this source are unavailable for the most recent period of the time series. However, this limitation does not substantially compromise the assessment, as the biological data spanning 1991–2011 encompass the full range of observed environmental and fishing conditions, providing sufficient temporal coverage to reliably estimate growth, selectivity, and size-structure dynamics that are propagated into subsequent model years through the population dynamics equations. Krill length compositions from AMLR surveys were processed following the same format as fishery-dependent data and handled according to stock assessment model template requirements. All data, together with the previously described ecosystem variables (environmental and predator-related), their temporal coverage, and their spatial scale, are summarized in Table \@ref(tab:data-sources) and illustrated in S1, Fig. 4.


```{r data-sources, eval=TRUE}
data <- data.frame(
  Source = c(
    "Krill Fishery Monitoring Program (CCAMLR SISO)",
    "Krill Fishery Monitoring Program (CCAMLR SISO)",
    "Krill Fishery Monitoring Program (CCAMLR SISO)",
    "AMLR Scientific Research Program",
    "AMLR Scientific Research Program",
    "Satellite Remote Sensing",
    "Predator Monitoring (MAPPPD)",
    "Predator Diet Composition (AMLR)"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Total reported catches",
    "Standardized CPUE (by vessel, area, and depth)",
    "Length-frequency distributions (0.2 cm bins)",
    "Biomass indices by MU from acoustic and net sampling",
    "Length-frequency distributions (0.2 cm bins)",
    "Monthly chlorophyll-a concentration from EU Copernicus Marine Service (interpolated; doi:10.48670/moi-00021, doi:10.48670/moi-00148)",
    "Abundance by colony-specific trends of penguin populations",
    "Length-frequency of krill in predator diets"
  ),
  TemporalScale = c(
    "1997–2020",
    "1997–2020",
    "2000–2020",
    "1991–2011 (based on cruise periods)",
    "1991–2011 (based on cruise periods)",
    "1997–present",
    "2000s–present",
    "1977–2020 (subset from 2000 used in this analysis)"
  ),
  SpatialScale = c(
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "By MU",
    "All Subarea 48.1",
    "By colony",
    "By species"
  )
)
# to word
ft_data <- flextable(data) %>%
  set_caption("Data sources used in the krill population dynamics modeling") %>%
  theme_vanilla() %>%
  fontsize(size = 9, part = "all") %>%
  font(fontname = "Arial", part = "all") %>%
  autofit() %>%
  set_table_properties(width = 1, layout = "autofit")

ft_data
```

\newpage

## Scenarios

A set of model configurations was evaluated, incorporating variations in predator "fleet" and environmental effects, and their impacts on spawning biomass, recruitment, and other population variables. The methodology involves assessing the statistical performance of the model under these configurations.  Specifically, `s1.1` serves as the reference model, incorporating only fishery and survey data while accounting for spatial heterogeneity (implicit spatial model). The subsequent scenarios build upon this configuration to evaluate the effects of additional ecological components. `s1.2` extends `s1.1` by incorporating predator influences, allowing the model to assess the impact of predation on krill population dynamics. `s1.3` modifies `s1.1` by introducing an environmental variable, capturing potential environmental drivers of recruitment. Finally, `s1.4` integrates both predator and environmental influences into the `s1.1` framework, providing a more complete representation of ecosystem interactions and their role in krill population dynamics. Regarding spatial heterogeneity, this approach was considered in all scenarios (Table \@ref(tab:scenario)).

```{r scenario, eval=TRUE}
scenario_table <- data.frame(
  Scenario    = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
  Description = c(
    "Base model",
    "Base model + predator mortality",
    "Base model + environmental covariate",
    "Base model + predator mortality + environmental covariate"
  ),
  Predator    = c("No", "Yes", "No",  "Yes"),
  Environment = c("No", "No",  "Yes", "Yes"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

ft_scenarios <- flextable(scenario_table) |>
  set_header_labels(
    Scenario    = "Scenario",
    Description = "Description",
    Predator    = "Predator (M\u2082)",
    Environment = "Chl-a link"
  ) |>
  set_caption("Model configurations evaluated for Antarctic krill stock assessment.") |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") |>
  bold(part = "header") |>
  align(j = c("Predator", "Environment"), align = "center", part = "all") |>
  autofit() |>
  set_table_properties(width = 1, layout = "autofit")

ft_scenarios
```
## Model Diagnostics
 
Regarding performance evaluation and scenario comparison, @Carvalho2017, @Carvalho2021b, @Merino2022a, and @Kell2021 propose a set of best practices for conducting a structured diagnostic assessment to ensure the reliability and robustness of stock assessment analyses. This diagnostic process was applied to the krill stock assessment and involved several key steps. First, convergence analysis was conducted to evaluate whether the estimation algorithm reached a stable solution. This included reviewing parameter trace plots, gradients, and the Hessian matrix to ensure that all parameters were well-estimated. Next, model fit to data was assessed by comparing observed and predicted values across key data sources, including catch, survey indices, and length composition. The *p*-values for the residual runs tests were computed for each of the four joint length compositions common to all models. The *p*-values were then combined into a single test statistic using Fisher's method [@Merino2022a]:

$$
\chi^2_{2k} = -2 \sum \log(p_i)
$$

where \( p_i \) is the p-value for length composition \( i \) and \( k \) denotes the degrees of freedom corresponding to the four *p*-values. Likelihood profiling was also carried out to quantify parameter uncertainty and identify the influence of priors. Pearson residuals of predicted length distributions for krill were evaluated across the four modeling scenarios to assess goodness-of-fit by standardizing the differences between observed and predicted values, allowing for the detection of systematic patterns or biases in model predictions across size classes and sampling sources. Hindcast cross-validation is another metric to evaluate model performance and evaluates model predictive skill by comparing one-step-ahead hindcast predictions against observed data using the Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE), which scales the model's mean absolute error relative to that of a naïve random-walk baseline. A MASE > 1 indicates that the model predicts no better than a random walk; MASE < 1 indicates genuine predictive skill; and MASE = 1 denotes equivalence with the naïve baseline. Retrospective analysis and hindcast cross-validation were performed by sequentially removing five years of data following @HurtadoF2015. As part of the retrospective analysis, the Mohn parameter was calculated as the mean relative error, defined as:

$$
\rho_M = \frac{1}{h} \sum_{y=1}^{h} \left( \frac{X_{T - y} - \hat{X}_{T - y}}{\hat{X}_{T - y}} \right)
$$

where \( X \) represents the quantities of interest (such as spawning stock biomass, SSB, or fishing mortality, F), \( \hat{X} \) is the corresponding estimate from the reference model fitted to the full dataset, \( T \) refers to the terminal assessment year, and \( h \) denotes the number of retrospective peels. This procedure involves removing recent years of data sequentially and re-estimating the model, allowing calculation of retrospective bias (*Rho*) for key metrics such as SSB and F. To evaluate statistical differences in model performance, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) values were extracted from the variance tuning diagnostics of each scenario and compiled for statistical analysis. To test for overall differences among scenarios, a one-way ANOVA [@Sthle1989] was conducted, treating RMSE values as the dependent variable and scenario configurations as the categorical factor. Model performance was also evaluated using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) [@Bozdogan1987], which balances model fit and complexity, facilitating comparisons across scenarios. This metrics penalizes model fit by twice the number of estimated parameters ($\text{AIC} = -2\ln\mathcal{L} + 2k$), thereby discouraging overparameterization [@Akaike1974]. Lower AIC values indicate more parsimonious models with better overall fit. The ACF of annual recruitment deviations was used to assess residual temporal structure in the estimated recruitment series across model configurations [@jhonson2016]. 

Table \@ref(tab:metrics) summarizes the diagnostic metrics used to evaluate model performance, including goodness-of-fit, prediction accuracy, information criteria, retrospective bias, and estimation stability.

```{r metrics, eval=TRUE}
df_model_metrics <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "Pearson Residuals",
    "Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)",
    "Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE)",
    "Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)",
    "Rho Parameter (B and SSB)",
    "ACF of Recruitment Deviations"
  ),
  Description = c(
    "Standardized residuals calculated as the difference between observed and expected values, divided by the standard deviation of the expected values. Useful for assessing model fit across data points.",
    "Measures the average magnitude of the residuals (prediction errors), giving higher weight to large errors. Lower values indicate better predictive accuracy.",
    "A scale-independent measure of prediction accuracy. It compares forecast errors to the errors from a naive baseline model. Values <1 indicate better-than-baseline performance.",
    "A relative measure of model quality that balances model fit and complexity. Lower AIC values indicate better models, penalizing overfitting.",
    "The autoregressive correlation coefficient from retrospective analysis, used to assess systematic bias in biomass (B) and spawning stock biomass (SSB) estimates across years.",
    "Autocorrelation function applied to annual recruitment deviations (RecDevs). Significant autocorrelation at successive lags indicates residual temporal structure not captured by the model, suggesting misspecification of recruitment dynamics."
  ),
  Reference = c(
    "—",
    "Carvalho et al. (2021)",
    "Hyndman & Koehler (2006)",
    "Bozdogan (1987)",
    "Hurtado-Ferro et al. (2015)",
    "Johnson et al. (2016)"
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

flextable(df_model_metrics) |>
  set_caption("Summary of model diagnostic metrics used for krill stock assessment.") |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  fontsize(size = 9, part = "all") |>
  font(fontname = "Arial", part = "all") |>
  bold(part = "header") |>
  autofit() |>
  set_table_properties(width = 1, layout = "autofit")
```


# Results

## Model Performance and Complexity Across Scenarios


All four scenarios achieved full convergence, with maximum gradient components below $1 \times 10^{-4}$ and positive-definite Hessian matrices (Table \@ref(tab:diagnostics)) confirming robust parameter estimation and local identifiability. Scenario `s1.1` represented the baseline spatial implicit model, incorporating only survey and fishery data, and resulted in the estimation of 94 parameters. In scenario `s1.2`, predator-related components were added, including biomass and size-selective predation patterns from key penguin colonies, increasing the parameter count to 102. Scenario `s1.3` introduced an environmental covariate as a recruitment driver into the baseline model, leading to the estimation of 98 parameters. Finally, scenario `s1.4` integrated both predator and environmental components, culminating in the highest model complexity with 104 estimated parameters. 

Pearson residuals from length composition data were examined as an initial diagnostic of model fit across all scenarios (S1, Fig 19 and Fig 20). All four scenarios produced residuals broadly centered around zero across fleets, years, and length bins, consistent with adequate convergence and absence of systematic directional bias. No scenario exhibited patterns of persistent over- or underestimation severe enough to question the overall model structure. In scenario s1.1, residuals across fishery and survey fleets are generally small and unstructured, with sporadic deviations concentrated in the larger size classes of certain survey fleets, particularly those operating in the Bransfield Strait. This baseline configuration captures the central tendency of observed length distributions reasonably well, though some residual signal remains unexplained in the tails of the size distribution. Scenario s1.3, which adds the environmental link to the baseline, shows a residual pattern closely resembling that of s1.1 across all shared fleets, reflecting the limited structural influence of the standardised environmental covariate on the fitted size compositions under this parameterisation. Scenarios s1.2 and s1.4, which incorporate the predator fleet, additionally show residuals associated with penguin stomach content length distributions. In s1.2, the predator fleet exhibits moderate to large residuals in certain years and size classes, suggesting that predation mortality alone introduces tension between the predator data and the remaining data streams. In s1.4, predator fleet residuals appear somewhat more balanced, with a more even distribution across years and size bins, tentatively suggesting that the joint inclusion of environmental and predator components provides a marginally more coherent fit to the full dataset. This pattern warrants further examination through complementary diagnostics.

RMSE patterns differed between data types and revealed a consistent trade-off across model configurations (Figure \@ref(fig:rmseplot)). For abundance indices, scenarios incorporating predator-derived natural mortality (s1.2, s1.4) achieved lower median RMSE and reduced interquartile ranges relative to the base model (s1.1) and the environmental-only configuration (s1.3), indicating improved fit to survey and fishery abundance trends. The opposite pattern was observed for length compositions, where s1.1 and s1.3 produced lower median RMSE and narrower dispersion than s1.2 and s1.4, suggesting that the additional structural complexity introduced by the predator term increases residual variability in size-structured fits. 

```{r rmseplot, eval=TRUE, fig.cap="Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) distributions across four model scenarios for abundance indices (left) and length compositions (right). Lower values indicate better model fit. Points represent fleet-specific RMSE values."}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/rmse_scenarios.png')
```

Hindcast cross-validation revealed consistent differences in predictive performance across configurations (Figure \@ref(fig:maseplt)). Scenario s1.2, incorporating predator-derived natural mortality, achieved the lowest mean MASE across all indices (2.11), followed by s1.4 (2.29), which combines predator mortality with the environmental covariate. The base model (s1.1; mean MASE = 2.72) and the environment-only configuration (s1.3; mean MASE = 2.74) showed the poorest predictive performance and were virtually indistinguishable from each other. Across all scenarios, mean MASE values exceeded 1, indicating that no configuration achieved systematic predictive skill superior to a random walk at the aggregate level. However, this result was strongly influenced by FISHERYGS, which consistently returned the highest individual MASE values (~8–9) across all scenarios, disproportionately inflating scenario-level means regardless of model structure. In contrast, acoustic survey indices (SURVEYEI, SURVEYGS) were the only components achieving MASE < 1 across all configurations, suggesting these data streams carry genuine one-step-ahead predictive signal.

```{r maseplt, eval=TRUE,  out.width="50%", fig.cap="Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) for krill population variables under scenarios of increasing ecosystem complexity"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/mase_hcxval_scenarios.png')
```

Retrospective analysis revealed divergent and in some cases extreme patterns across scenarios and quantities (Figure \@ref(fig:rhoplot). For spawning stock biomass (SSB), s1.1 and s1.3 (environment only) showed positive combined Mohn's $\rho$ (0.119 and 0.446, respectively), indicating a systematic tendency toward overestimation when data from recent years are progressively removed. Scenario s1.3 was particularly notable, with individual peel deviations exceeding 3 in the 2016 peel, suggesting that the environmental covariate introduces structural instability in SSB estimation under data reduction. Scenarios s1.2 (predator only) and s1.4 produced negative combined SSB $\rho$ ($-$0.220 and $-$0.629), reflecting retrospective underestimation, with s1.4 reaching approximately $-$0.75 in the 2017 peel. For fishing mortality (F), s1.1, s1.2, and s1.3 all showed negative combined $\rho$ values (ranging from $-$0.869 to $-$0.954), indicating a consistent tendency to overestimate F in terminal years relative to earlier peels, though without extreme individual deviations. Scenario s1.4 departed markedly from this pattern, producing a strongly positive combined $\rho$ of 1.754 and an extreme spike in the 2017 peel ($\rho$ > 3), indicating severe retrospective instability in F when predator mortality and environmental covariates are incorporated simultaneously (Table \@ref(tab:diagnostics) and S1 Table 3 to 10). This spike suggests that the 2017 peel year is a leverage point for the joint ecosystem configuration, likely reflecting the sensitivity of F estimation to the interaction between predator-driven mortality and environmental recruitment signals in a data-limited peel context. The full retrospective trajectories for SSB and F across all peels and scenarios are provided in Supplementary Material (S1, Fig 23 and Fig 24 respectively).

```{r rhoplot, eval=TRUE, out.width="75%", fig.cap="Retrospective patterns for fishing mortality (F, left) and spawning stock biomass (SSB, right) across four modeling scenarios differing in ecosystem complexity. Each point represents the rho value for a given terminal year (2016–2019), with Combined indicating the average retrospective bias across years in different scenarios"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/rhoplot.png')
```

The autocorrelation of annual recruitment deviations was used to assess residual temporal structure in the estimated recruitment series across model configurations (Figure \@ref(fig:acfrecruit)) . Scenarios s1.1 and s1.3 exhibited recruitment deviations largely uncorrelated across lags, with all autocorrelation coefficients remaining within the 95% confidence bounds. Scenario s1.2 showed moderate autocorrelation at intermediate lags, without systematic exceedance of significance thresholds. Scenario s1.4 displayed the highest degree of autocorrelation, with the coefficient at lag 1 exceeding the significance threshold and several subsequent lags exhibiting notable structure.

```{r acfrecruit, eval=TRUE, fig.cap="Autocorrelation function (ACF) of recruitment deviations for four krill population model scenarios. Blue dashed lines indicate 95% confidence intervals"}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/ACF_recruitment.png')
```


The stock-recruitment relationship (SRR) for Antarctic krill revealed consistent and biologically coherent differences across model configurations. Scenarios excluding predator-derived natural mortality (s1.1, s1.3) produced substantially higher asymptotic recruitment curves (~40–45 billion individuals), with predicted  values remaining elevated even at low spawning biomass levels, suggesting high compensatory productivity under these assumptions (S1, Fig 18-A). In contrast, configurations incorporating the M2 predator term (s1.2, s1.4) yielded markedly lower and more saturating SRR curves (~10–15 billion), implying reduced population productivity when predation mortality is explicitly accounted for. Recruitment efficiency (R/SSB) as a function of relative spawning biomass (SSB/SSB~0~) also differed markedly: s1.1 and s1.3 consistently exhibited higher per-capita productivity across all depletion levels, while s1.2 and s1.4 show substantially lower efficiency, particularly at low SSB (S1, Fig 18-B). The virgin recruitment parameter ln(R~0~) confirms this grouping: s1.1 and s1.3 estimate higher unfished recruitment (~24.3–24.5), whereas s1.2 and s1.4 converge on lower values (~23.4), with s1.4 showing the most precise estimate (narrowest posterior) (S1, Fig 18-C).

Taken together, these diagnostics suggest a consistent trade-off between ecosystem realism and statistical parsimony. To formalize this comparison, model complexity was evaluated using the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC). s1.3 provided the best parsimony-adjusted fit (1,631.4), closely followed by s1.1 (1,640.4). In contrast, s1.2 (2,531.8) and s1.4 (2,655.1) showed substantially higher values, indicating that the structural cost of incorporating predator-derived mortality, reflected in the additional parameters required to estimate time-varying $M_2$, predator selectivity, and associated deviations, was not offset by a commensurate improvement in likelihood. The $\Delta$AIC between the predator-inclusive scenarios and the base model exceeded 890 units, a difference well beyond conventional significance thresholds [@Burnham2002], providing strong evidence that the additional complexity introduced by $M_2$ is not supported by the data in information-theoretic terms, though it should be noted that AIC comparisons across model configurations fitted to non-identical data streams, as is the case here, where predator-inclusive scenarios incorporate additional observational components, should be interpreted with caution, as differences in the likelihood basis partly reflect data structure rather than model parsimony alone (Table \@ref(tab:diagnostics).

```{r diagnostics, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
diagnostics <- read_csv("outputs/model_diagnosis_results.csv")
grad_row <- which(diagnostics$Description == "Final gradient")
param_row <- which(diagnostics$Description == "No. parameters")

flextable(diagnostics) |>
  colformat_num(j      = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
                digits = 2,
                na_str = "-") |>
  compose(
    i     = grad_row,
    j     = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
    value = as_paragraph("<1.00e-04")
  ) |>
  compose(
    i     = param_row,
    j     = c("s1.1", "s1.2", "s1.3", "s1.4"),
    value = as_paragraph(
      as_chunk(format(as.integer(diagnostics[param_row, c("s1.1","s1.2","s1.3","s1.4")]),
                      big.mark = ""))
    )
  ) |>
  set_header_labels(Description = "Metric") |>
  set_caption("Statistical components to evaluate performance and diagnostics of model scenarios") |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  fontsize(size = 10, part = "all") |>
  bold(part = "header") |>
  autofit() |>
  set_table_properties(width = 1, layout = "autofit")
```

## Comparison between scenarios

Krill population variables showed consistent temporal trends across all scenarios over the period 1989–2020, differing primarily in magnitude rather than in the direction or timing of population dynamics (Figure \@ref(fig:compara1)). Four biomass metrics are reported: total biomass ($B_{all}$), which includes all individuals at the start of each year; summary biomass ($B_{smry}$), which excludes individuals below the minimum summary age and represents the fraction vulnerable to surveys and the fishery; spawning stock biomass (SSB), computed at the time of peak spawning (austral summer); and age-0 recruitment. Survey-aggregated biomass estimates from the AMLR hydroacoustic Program [@Dornam2021] (blue diamonds; 95% CI propagated across management units) are overlaid on the $B_{smry}$ panel, as SS3 calibrates survey catchability $q$ against this metric. The apparent discrepancy between raw survey totals and model-estimated $B_{smry}$ is expected: an estimated $q > 1$ indicates that surveys sampled areas of above-average krill density relative to the stock-wide mean [@Everson2000; @Siegel2005], such that the sum of observations across management units represents a spatially biased index rather than an absolute stock estimate, a scaling resolved internally within the SS3 likelihood framework. SSB may exceed $B_{smry}$ in years of rapid somatic growth between the start of the year and the spawning event, consistent with the fast growth rates characteristic of Antarctic krill [@Atkinson2006]. All four scenarios tracked the same interannual dynamics: a period of relatively stable biomass from 1989 to approximately 2010, followed by a marked increase peaking around 2015–2016, and a partial decline thereafter. Recruitment (age-0) showed high interannual variability across all scenarios, consistent with the episodic and environmentally driven nature of krill recruitment [@Dornam2021; @Dong2025], with a pronounced peak in 2015 shared by all configurations. These common trends indicate that the underlying population signal is robust across model structures. Differences among scenarios were, however, substantial in magnitude: configurations excluding predator-derived natural mortality (s1.1, s1.3) consistently produced higher estimates across all variables, with biomass and recruitment peaks approximately two to five times greater than those estimated under s1.2 and s1.4  (S1. Fig 17). The most constrained estimates were observed under s1.4, where predator mortality and environmental covariates operate simultaneously on both recruitment and natural mortality.


```{r compara1, eval=TRUE,  out.width="100%", fig.cap = "Time series of krill population variables in the Western Antarctic Peninsula (WAP): total biomass, summary biomass, spawning stock biomass, and recruitment (left to right). Blue diamonds represent survey biomass estimates from the AMLR Program across Subarea 48.1."}
knitr::include_graphics('Figs/popvar_scenarios.png')
```


# Discusion

This study advances understanding of Antarctic krill (*Euphausia superba*) population dynamics by addressing two fundamental objectives. First, it consolidates and harmonizes multiple long-term datasets collected over decades, particularly from Subarea 48.1 in the Western Antarctic Peninsula, into a unified statistical framework, responding to a recurrent management priority regarding integrated models for krill emphasized by CCAMLR. Second, it integrates ecosystem variables such as environmental drivers and predator impacts, thereby moving beyond the traditional single-stock approach and taking a step toward ecosystem-informed stock assessments. This approach not only enhances the biological realism of krill population modeling but also reveals the structural complexity and statistical challenges that must be addressed for its effective application in Antarctic fishery management contexts.

Prior integrated assessments of Antarctic krill have progressively advanced the structural complexity of population models, moving from age-structured formulations [@Kinzey2015] to size-structured frameworks [@Wang2021; @Green2023; @Zhang2022; @Dong2025], all implemented within Stock Synthesis or analogous platforms. Despite these advances, two fundamental limitations persist across this body of work. First, these assessments have relied on a restricted set of input data — typically conditioned on acoustic survey indices and fishery catch, without simultaneously incorporating fishery-dependent size compositions, standardized CPUE, predator abundance indices, diet-derived size selectivity, and environmental covariates within the same likelihood framework. Second, and more critically, ecosystem drivers such as environmental variability and predator pressure have been treated as external to the core population dynamics — explored through sensitivity analyses or post-hoc diagnostics rather than embedded structurally within the equations governing recruitment and natural mortality. As a result, their influence on estimated stock status and management advice has remained indirect and largely non-binding. The present study departs from both limitations: it conditions the model simultaneously on the full breadth of available data streams — fishery-dependent data, acoustic survey indices, a primary productivity covariate linked to recruitment, and penguin abundance and diet composition informing predation mortality — and embeds these ecosystem components directly within the state equations. This dual integration, of data sources and ecological structure, represents the primary methodological contribution of this work and the key distinction from all previous krill assessments.

## Spatial Considerations and the Areas-as-Fleets Approach

Recognizing and accounting for spatial heterogeneity in krill populations across Subarea 48.1 was essential for the integrated model design. The areas-as-fleets approach captures spatial differences in abundance, selectivity, and population processes, allowing their incorporation into global population estimates [@Nielsen2021; @Waterhouse2014]. However, some management units,  particularly Joinville Island, had limited survey and fishery monitoring data, which could introduce complications in future integrated krill modeling efforts. Moving toward spatially explicit integrated models represents the natural next step, as it would allow movement, local depletion, and habitat-driven heterogeneity to be represented directly within the population dynamics rather than absorbed by fleet-specific parameters. State-space frameworks such as SAM [@Nielsen2021] and WHAM [@Stock2021] offer structural advantages in this direction, particularly in their capacity to partition process and observation uncertainty and to accommodate time-varying spatial effects. However, both frameworks rely on age-structured population dynamics and require age-composition data for reliable process error estimation, a fundamental constraint for krill, given the absence of hard aging structures and the practical infeasibility of direct age determination at assessment scales. Spatially explicit implementation for krill therefore remains contingent on advances in aging methodology or the development of length-structured state-space extensions capable of resolving spatial population processes without relying on age data.

<!-- Falta contexto sobre modelos espacialmente explícitos para krill. Dos papers directamente relevantes: -->



<!-- KRILLPODYM (@Green2023) — modelo mecanístico espacialmente resuelto para krill, distribución y abundancia. Cita natural cuando dices "el próximo paso son modelos espacialmente explícitos". -->
<!-- Freer et al. (2025) — modelo de distribución dinámica para krill con covariables ambientales en South Scotia Sea. Refuerza la viabilidad del enfoque espacial explícito. -->
<!-- Atkinson et al. (2022, Frontiers) — marco de riesgo espacial para distribuir el límite de captura de krill en 48.1. Directamente relevante para el argumento de gestión espacial. -->

## Ecosystems Drivers and Recruitment Dynamics

The identification of Chl-a concentration as the primary environmental driver affecting krill recruitment highlights the central role of primary productivity in shaping population dynamics [@Ainley2017; @Constable2023; @Saba2014]. This finding is consistent with studies emphasizing that krill recruitment is primarily driven by environmental conditions rather than spawning stock biomass [@ichii2023; @Perry2020; @Veytia2021]. By explicitly incorporating variables mechanistically linked to recruitment, ecosystem-informed models move beyond purely stochastic representations of stock productivity [@Haltuch2009; @Crone2019]. However, the environmental covariate framework, while effective in capturing Chl-a–driven recruitment variability, represents a simplified view of the complex environmental drivers affecting krill populations. Future studies should consider multivariate environmental relationships, threshold effects, and lagged responses that may better represent mechanistic links between climate variability and krill population dynamics.

The absence of predictive improvement in s1.3 relative to s1.1 in terms of MASE does not imply ecological irrelevance of primary productivity as a recruitment driver, but rather that the additive Z-scored link as currently parameterised does not translate that relationship into improved predictive capacity. Similar limitations have been noted in ecosystem-informed stock assessments where environmental covariates are incorporated additively without sufficient contrast in the covariate time series to generate identifiable recruitment effects [@Punt2014]. In SS3, recruitment deviations absorb environmental forcing when estimated simultaneously with covariate links, which likely explains the near-identical predictive performance of s1.3 and s1.1. State-space models such as WHAM [@Stock2021] explicitly partition process and observation uncertainty, allowing environmental covariates to act on latent state variables without competing with random effects , an architecture shown to improve retrospective diagnostics in ecosystem-informed assessments [@Kittel2026]. However, applying state-space frameworks to Antarctic krill faces fundamental data limitations: these models typically require age-structured data to estimate process errors reliably, whereas krill lack hard ageing structures, making direct age determination impractical and rendering age-based state-space approaches currently unfeasible for this species.

<!-- Falta respaldo para la competencia entre recruitment deviations y covariables en SS3. Paper clave: -->

<!-- Jacobson et al. (2019, Fisheries Research) — "Good practices for including environmental data in integrated stock assessments" — encuentra que incluir covariables dentro de la función stock-recluta (como haces en s1.3) produce mayor sesgo que usarla como índice externo. Respaldo directo para tu interpretación de por qué s1.3 no mejora MASE. -->
<!-- Kittel et al. (2026) — ya lo tienes citado, úsalo también aquí. -->



The integration of penguin predation data into natural mortality estimates represents a novel approach in an integrated stock assessment, and particularly so for krill. The size-selective predation pattern incorporated is biologically consistent with foraging theory and observed predator behaviour; no significant differences in krill size ranges were identified among key *Pygoscelis* predator species in their diet [@Panasiuk2024]. The declining trend in the synoptic predator biomass index after 2015 reflects documented population declines in Antarctic Peninsula penguin colonies [@Emmerson2022; @Kruger2019; @Salmeron2023; @Trivelpiece2011a], attributed to climate change impacts, sea ice loss, and shifts in prey availability [@Kruger2019]. The incorporation of this declining predation pressure provides a mechanism for capturing ecosystem-mediated changes in natural mortality that would otherwise be overlooked in traditional single-species approaches.

Incorporating predation mortality into stock assessment models has precedent in the literature. @Hollowed2000 revealed higher total natural mortality rates than traditional life-history estimates and showed that ignoring predation uncertainty underestimated stock biomass uncertainty by up to 20%. Similarly, @Richards2016 introduced a predation pressure index for northern shrimp, combining predator biomass and diet frequency to scale natural mortality over time, improving model fit and reducing retrospective bias without requiring absolute predator consumption data. Other predators such as whales [@Pallin2023; @Reisinger2022], which may exert substantially greater impact on krill abundance, were not included in this analysis due to high variability in methods and estimates across available time series in the WAP. Studies suggest that whale consumption rates may exceed total krill catch by approximately 20-fold [@Biuw2024] in certain years and sub-areas, highlighting the necessity of incorporating these key predators in future work to more accurately represent predation mortality.

<!-- Falta un paralelo en otra especie para el M2 framework. Paper relevante: -->

<!-- Johannessen et al. (2024, Fisheries Management and Ecology) — "Sensitivity of the stock assessment for Antarctic krill to time-varying natural and fishing mortality" — directamente sobre krill y M natural variable. Muy relevante para la discusión de M2. -->
<!-- ICES JMS 2025 — predación de mamíferos marinos sobre Pacific Herring implementada en evaluación integrada — paralelo metodológico para tu uso de M2. -->

<!-- ## Biomass Trajectories and Productivity -->

The systematic divergence between predator-inclusive and predator-exclusive configurations is consistent with the well-documented role of seabird and marine mammal predation as a major source of krill mortality in the WAP. Predator consumption of krill in this region has been estimated to substantially exceed commercial catch removals, and its omission from population models is expected to produce upward-biased biomass estimates [@Murphy2007; @Constable2000]. The grouping pattern observed , in which the presence or absence of M2 predator mortality, rather than environmental covariates, determines the level of estimated biomass , is consistent with findings showing that top-down predation pressure exerts a stronger and more continuous influence on krill population size than bottom-up environmental variability, which tends to operate episodically through recruitment [@Atkinson2004].

The pronounced biomass and recruitment peaks estimated for 2015–2016 across all scenarios are consistent with anomalously favourable environmental conditions documented for this period in the WAP, including above-average chlorophyll-a concentrations and sea-ice dynamics conducive to krill spawning success [@Siegel2005]. In the absence of predator mortality, the model attributes the full observed signal in survey indices to population growth rather than partitioning it between biological production and predation-driven removal. This confounding effect has direct implications for reference point estimation: biomass trajectories that do not account for predation will tend to overstate stock productivity and recovery potential [@Constable2000; @Kinzey2019]. These results are broadly consistent with @Zhang2022, who also found that ecosystem-informed configurations produced higher biomass estimates under certain parameterizations , though the direction of this effect depends critically on whether predation or environmental productivity dominates the ecosystem signal being incorporated.

## Model Performance and Diagnostic Evaluation

All scenarios achieved convergence and produced stable Hessian matrices, confirming that the increasing structural complexity of ecosystem-informed configurations did not compromise numerical stability. The comprehensive diagnostic evaluation, however, revealed important and consistent trade-offs between biological realism and statistical performance [@Collie2016; @Robertson2024].

Based on AIC, s1.3 provided the best parsimony-adjusted fit (1,631.4), closely followed by s1.1 (1,636.9), while s1.2 (2,531.8) and s1.4 (2,655.1) showed substantially higher values, indicating that the addition of predator-derived mortality incurred a considerable informational cost not offset by improved overall fit. This pattern was driven primarily by length composition likelihoods, which were markedly lower in s1.1 and s1.3 (~393–394) than in s1.2 and s1.4 (~908–949), indicating substantially poorer fit to length frequency data in predator-inclusive configurations. In contrast, survey likelihood was lowest in s1.2 (290.2) and s1.4 (306.8), suggesting that predator scenarios achieved better alignment with abundance index trends despite their overall poorer fit. This divergence between compositional and index fit is consistent with the risk of overfitting compositional data at the expense of capturing the broader population trajectory [@Punt2013; @Maunder2013], and reflects a fundamental tension between data types in integrated assessments.

<!-- Model Performance — AIC con datos no idénticos -->
<!-- Falta cita que respalde el caveat que te sugerí añadir. Paper encontrado: -->

<!-- Citado en la revisión de validación de métodos de evaluación (ICES JMS, 2021) — establece explícitamente: "model selection based on AIC is only suitable for comparing frameworks based on the same input data." Cita: Carvalho et al. (2021), ICES Journal of Marine Science — ya lo tienes en el manuscrito como @Carvalho2021b, añádelo aquí también. -->

The hindcast cross-validation (HCxval) diagnostic provides a robust framework for comparing model configurations beyond in-sample goodness-of-fit metrics [@Carvalho2021]. MASE values exceeding 1.0 across all scenarios reflect the extreme interannual variability inherent in Antarctic krill abundance [@Atkinson2004; @Everson2000], representing a fundamental challenge for any deterministic assessment framework. The persistent poor predictive performance of the FISHERYGS index (MASE ≈ 8–9) is consistent with the known spatial aggregation behaviour of krill, which generates high variability in fishery-dependent indices difficult to capture through model structure alone [@Everson2000; @Constable2000]. The relative improvement in predictive skill in s1.2 and s1.4 suggests that temporally structured variation in M2, informed by MAPPPD penguin abundance indices, introduces ecologically relevant information that partially improves the model's ability to track observed abundance trends , consistent with the role of top-down predator control in structuring krill dynamics at sub-seasonal to interannual timescales [@Trivelpiece2011a; @Constable2000].

The deterioration in retrospective performance in ecosystem-informed scenarios reflects the informational cost incurred when predator and environmental drivers are embedded directly within the state equations. The extreme instability of F estimates in s1.4 (Mohn's $\rho$ = 1.754) is consistent with identifiability constraints arising from the joint estimation of time-varying predation and fishing mortality from the same population signal. The extreme SSB $\rho$ in s1.3 (0.446) reinforces the finding that the environmental link, as currently parameterised, introduces instability into biomass estimates without providing a stabilising ecological constraint. These patterns should not be interpreted as evidence against the ecological relevance of the drivers incorporated, but rather as a consequence of imposing structural constraints on a data-limited system , a trade-off central to the evaluation presented here [@HurtadoF2015].


Pearson residual misfits concentrated among larger size classes in several Bransfield Strait survey fleets suggest that the current spatially implicit model structure may not fully resolve the processes governing adult krill abundance and selectivity in this sub-region. The Bransfield Strait has been consistently identified as a core juvenile habitat [@Mardones2025; @Veytia2021; @Perry2020; @Perry2019], implying that adult individuals are disproportionately distributed elsewhere. Incorporating explicit spatial structure in future assessments could reduce these residual patterns by allowing size-selective processes to vary across strata. Similarly, persistent residuals in the largest size classes of the predator fleet reflect limitations in the time-invariant logistic selectivity function applied to penguin predation, which may inadequately capture temporal shifts in prey size preferences [@Marin2001; @Silvestro2023].

Autocorrelated recruitment deviations in s1.4 indicate that this configuration failed to fully capture the underlying drivers of interannual recruitment variability [@Thorson2014; @jhonson2016]. When both $M_2(t)$ and $\beta_E E_t$ are estimated jointly, collinearity between ecosystem drivers prevents the optimiser from cleanly partitioning mortality and recruitment signals, forcing recruitment deviations to compensate for unresolved variance. This pattern reinforces the interpretation that s1.4 incurs a higher informational cost and poorer internal consistency than simpler configurations.

<!-- Falta umbral explícito para krill. Paper crítico: -->

<!-- Breivik et al. (2023, ICES Journal of Marine Science) — "Misinterpreting retrospective patterns in fishery stock assessment" — argumento central: los umbrales de Mohn's ρ son dependientes de los datos y la complejidad del modelo, no universales. Perfecta cita de apoyo para contextualizar los valores extremos de s1.4 sin tener que descartarlos automáticamente. -->
<!-- Ya tienes @HurtadoF2015 con los umbrales para especies de vida corta (ρ > 0.30 o < −0.22 para sardina). Usa ambos en conjunto. -->

## Implications for Precautionary Management

Despite concerns about the statistical performance of ecosystem-informed models, their estimates of key population variables , including spawning biomass, total biomass, and recruitment , are consistently lower than those produced by models without ecosystem variables. The significant reduction in spawning biomass between s1.1 and s1.4 (mean difference: 2.6 million tonnes) demonstrates that neglecting ecosystem variables may lead to overly optimistic stock assessments and potentially unsustainable harvest recommendations. This conservative tendency aligns with CCAMLR's strategic guidelines calling for the integration of ecosystem variables into predictive models to support precautionary management [@Constable2011; @Cavanagh2016; @Constable2023]. The more conservative estimates produced by ecosystem-informed models provide a buffer against uncertainty in population status, which is particularly important given the high variability and unpredictability of Antarctic marine ecosystems [@Hill2016]. However, the increased model complexity and associated statistical challenges must be balanced against the improved biological realism to ensure that management advice remains scientifically credible and operationally feasible.

<!-- El contexto de gestión ha cambiado dramáticamente desde tu análisis. Paper clave: -->

<!-- PNAS 2025 — "Adjusting the management of the Antarctic krill fishery to meet the challenges of the 21st century" — directamente relevante para justificar por qué modelos ecosystem-informed son necesarios ahora. -->
<!-- El vencimiento de la Medida de Conservación 51-07 en 2024 y el récord de capturas en 2025 hacen tu argumento de precaución aún más urgente — puedes mencionarlo en Implications como contexto actualizado. -->

## Model Limitations and Future Directions

Several limitations of the current modelling approach warrant discussion. The integration of predator effects relied on relatively coarse-scale abundance indices that may not fully capture fine-scale predator–prey interactions across the 658,739 km² extent of Subarea 48.1, where CCAMLR's ecosystem observer deployment does not provide spatially comprehensive coverage. The current model also assumes static relationships between ecosystem variables and parameter estimates; climate change may alter these relationships over time, requiring adaptive modelling approaches that can accommodate non-stationary ecosystem conditions [@Constable2023]. The development of time-varying parameter frameworks or regime-specific models may be necessary to address this challenge.

Perfomance and diagnostic limitations reflect the inherent cost of imposing ecosystem structure within a spatially implicit single-species framework. Rather than indicating fundamental model failure, they identify specific processes, spatial heterogeneity in size structure and temporally dynamic predation selectivity, that represent natural targets for future model development. Despite these limitations, this study represents a forward-looking direction for integrated krill assessments, providing a scientifically grounded foundation for recommendations to CCAMLR employing the most advanced fisheries science methods currently applied in comparable systems worldwide.


\newpage

# Conclusion


This study demonstrates that integrating ecosystem variables into Antarctic krill stock assessment models produces measurable and consistent effects on key population variables, with important implications for management advice. By harmonizing diverse long-term datasets within a spatially implicit integrated framework and embedding environmental and predator covariates directly within the state equations, we show that the explicit representation of ecosystem components generates systematic and coherent differences in estimated population variables. Predation-derived natural mortality emerged as the dominant structural driver of these differences, producing substantially lower and more conservative estimates of spawning biomass, total biomass, and recruitment relative to configurations that excluded top-down control. Environmental forcing through primary productivity modulated recruitment dynamics but exerted a comparatively weaker influence on the overall level of population variables, with its main contribution being the introduction of interannual variability around the baseline trajectory rather than a shift in mean productivity. Together, these results indicate that the choice of which ecosystem components to embed within the core population dynamics — and how — determines not only the magnitude but also the temporal structure of estimated stock status. These outcomes align with CCAMLR's precautionary management principles, and suggest that assessments excluding ecosystem processes may overestimate population productivity and stock status. At the same time, the increased structural complexity introduced by predator and environmental components incurs measurable statistical costs — including retrospective instability and autocorrelated recruitment deviations — that must be transparently evaluated rather than ignored.

Looking forward, future work should prioritize improving predator–prey datasets to extend temporal and spatial coverage of predation mortality, explore multivariate and non-linear environmental relationships capable of capturing threshold and lagged responses in recruitment, and develop length-structured state-space frameworks that can accommodate ecosystem covariates without relying on age-structured data. These advances are not merely methodological — they are operationally urgent. The lapse of Conservation Measure 51-07 in 2024 and the record krill catches reported in 2025 underscore the need for assessment frameworks that can credibly represent ecosystem dynamics and support spatially explicit, precautionary harvest decisions. This work provides a reproducible and scientifically grounded foundation for that effort within CCAMLR's evolving management strategy for Subarea 48.1.


\newpage

# Acknowledgments

This research was supported by the following funding sources: the INACH "Marine Protected Areas" Program (Grant No. 2409052), the ANID/Millennium Science Initiative Program (Grant No. ICN2021_002), the CCAMLR Scholarship Scheme (2023-2024), and the Doctorate Scholarship from CENTRO-IDEAL at the Universidad Austral de Chile. The authors extend their gratitude to the Secretariat of the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR) for providing access to the krill fishery data, which was instrumental to this study.

# Supplementary Material 

-   Supplementary Material 1: Reproducible Code to Output and Model Diagnostics for Antarctic Krill Stock Assessment. Code and Scenarios to reproduce this analysis can be found in [SA_Krill_Repo](https://mauromardones.github.io/SA_Krill/). 

-   Supplementary Material 2: Mathematical Formulas and parameters used in SS3 for Krill Stock Assessment



<!-- # Data Availability -->

<!-- Data will be made available on request. -->

\newpage

# References
